posts.title: agents aren't replacing devs, they're replacing context switching
posts.hook: the biggest productivity killer in engineering isn't bad code. it's the 23 minutes it takes to regain focus after switching tabs.
posts.body:
the biggest productivity killer in engineering isn't bad code. it's the 23 minutes it takes to regain focus after switching tabs.

I started running Claude Code agents in parallel last month. not for writing code. for holding context.

one agent watches the build. one agent tracks test failures. one agent monitors the deploy pipeline. I stay in a single file doing the actual thinking.

the result isn't faster code generation. it's fewer interruptions.

before this, my workflow was: write code, switch to terminal, check build, switch to browser, check deploy, switch to Slack, answer question, switch back to editor... forget where I was.

now I write code. agents surface problems to me. I fix them without leaving my editor.

the discourse around AI agents is all about replacement. will they replace junior devs? will they replace QA?

wrong question.

the right question: what if agents just let humans hold deeper focus for longer?

that's the unlock nobody's talking about. not more output. deeper output.
posts.cta: what's the context switch that kills your flow the most?

remixes.label: tactical-breakdown
remixes.hook: I stopped alt-tabbing and my output doubled. Not because I got faster. Because I stopped losing 23 minutes every time I switched windows.
remixes.body:
I stopped alt-tabbing and my output doubled. Not because I got faster. Because I stopped losing 23 minutes every time I switched windows.

The research is clear. Context switching is the single most expensive cognitive tax in engineering. Every tab switch, every Slack ping, every "quick question" resets your mental stack.

So I built a system where I never leave my editor.

Three Claude Code agents run in parallel terminals. One watches CI. One monitors test output. One tracks deploy status. They surface only what matters. I read a one-line summary instead of switching to three different dashboards.

The shift isn't about AI writing code for me. It's about AI being my peripheral vision.

Before this setup, I'd lose 45 minutes a day just checking build status. Now that information comes to me. I stay in flow state for 2-3 hour blocks instead of 20 minute fragments.

The math is simple. If you context switch 15 times a day and each costs you 10 minutes of ramp-up, that's 2.5 hours of dead time. Every single day.

Agents didn't make me a better engineer. They made me an uninterrupted one.
remixes.cta: how many times did you switch tabs in the last hour? count it tomorrow. the number will surprise you.

remixes.label: contrarian-flip
remixes.hook: The 10x engineer myth was always wrong. It was never about typing speed. It was about who could hold the most context in their head without breaking.
remixes.body:
The 10x engineer myth was always wrong. It was never about typing speed. It was about who could hold the most context in their head without breaking.

The best engineers I've worked with had one thing in common. They could zoom in on a problem for hours without surfacing. Not because they were smarter. Because their environment protected their focus.

Now that protection is available to everyone.

I run AI agents as context holders. Not code generators. One agent holds the state of my test suite. One holds the deployment pipeline. One holds the PR review queue. I query them when I need to, like checking a dashboard that talks back.

This changes who can be "10x." It's no longer about raw cognitive horsepower. It's about whether you set up systems that protect your attention.

The engineer who builds their own focus infrastructure will outperform the genius who's drowning in notifications every time.

We spent 20 years optimizing code execution. Turns out the bottleneck was always human execution. Specifically, the interrupt-driven way we work.
remixes.cta: who's the most focused engineer you've ever worked with? what did they do differently?

tones.advisor:
The research on context switching costs is well established. 23 minutes to regain deep focus after an interruption. For engineering teams, this is the single largest source of lost productivity, and most orgs don't even measure it.

Here's what I recommend. Stop thinking of AI agents as code generators. Start thinking of them as context holders.

The setup I've validated: run three parallel agents. One monitors your build pipeline. One tracks test failures in real time. One watches deploy status. You stay in your editor. You stay in flow.

The ROI calculation is straightforward. If your engineers context switch 10-15 times per day, and each switch costs 10-20 minutes of cognitive ramp-up, you're burning 2-4 hours of deep work per person per day. Multiply that across your team.

This isn't about replacing developers. It's about removing the cognitive overhead that prevents them from doing their best work. The agents handle monitoring. The humans handle thinking.

I've been running this setup for a month. The measurable result: longer uninterrupted blocks, fewer dropped threads, and faster incident response because the agent surfaces the problem before I even know to check.
tones.provocateur:
You're not slow because you're a bad engineer. You're slow because your tools force you to be a human task scheduler.

Every time you check the build, you pay a tax. Every time you flip to the deploy dashboard, tax. Every Slack thread about a failing test, tax. 23 minutes of focus destroyed per switch. And you're switching dozens of times a day.

But sure, keep blaming it on "technical debt" in your sprint retros.

I stopped pretending willpower was the answer. I set up Claude Code agents to hold the context I kept dropping. Build status. Test results. Deploy state. Three agents, three terminals, zero tab switches.

Now I write code in 3-hour blocks. Uninterrupted. While the agents watch everything I used to manually check.

The dirty secret of engineering productivity isn't better frameworks or faster CI. It's that most engineers spend half their day doing the cognitive equivalent of spinning in an office chair between three desks.

We built notification systems, monitoring dashboards, and Slack integrations. Then we wonder why nobody can focus. The tooling is the problem. Not the people.

posts.title: the GTM engineer role doesn't exist on job boards yet
posts.hook: I keep seeing companies post for 'RevOps Manager' when what they actually need is someone who can write SQL, build Clay tables, and ship outbound sequences by Thursday.
posts.body:
I keep seeing companies post for 'RevOps Manager' when what they actually need is someone who can write SQL, build Clay tables, and ship outbound sequences by Thursday.

that role has a name. it's GTM engineer. most companies just haven't caught up.

here's the pattern I see over and over:

1. company hires a RevOps person
2. RevOps person is great at process design and Salesforce admin
3. company also needs someone to build enrichment pipelines, write API integrations, and automate personalization at scale
4. RevOps person drowns

the gap isn't effort. it's skillset.

GTM engineering sits at the intersection of sales ops, data engineering, and marketing automation. you need to understand why an SDR skips a lead AND how to build the scoring model that prevents it.

I built this function from scratch at my last company. no playbook. no job description to copy. just problems that needed solving and tools that could solve them.

the teams that figure out this role early are running circles around teams still debating whether outbound is dead.
posts.cta: if you're building a GTM eng function, drop a comment. I'll share the exact stack and org chart that worked.

remixes.label: job-market-gap
remixes.hook: There are thousands of people doing GTM engineering work right now. They just have the wrong title on LinkedIn.
remixes.body:
There are thousands of people doing GTM engineering work right now. They just have the wrong title on LinkedIn.

They're called "RevOps Manager" or "Growth Lead" or "Marketing Ops." But what they actually do is write SQL queries, build enrichment pipelines in Clay, configure Salesforce automations, and ship outbound sequences on deadline.

That's not operations. That's engineering applied to go-to-market.

The gap is real. Companies need someone who can look at a target account list and, by end of week, have a working pipeline that enriches contacts, scores them, personalizes outreach, and pushes qualified leads into sequences.

A traditional RevOps hire designs processes and maintains systems. A GTM engineer builds the systems from scratch.

I've seen this play out at four companies now. They hire RevOps. RevOps is excellent at Salesforce admin and process documentation. Then leadership asks for a custom enrichment workflow or a Clay-to-HubSpot pipeline, and the RevOps person is stuck because that's an engineering problem.

The role exists. The title is catching up. And the people who claim it early will have their pick of opportunities.
remixes.cta: are you doing GTM engineering under a different title? what do they call your role?

remixes.label: hiring-playbook
remixes.hook: The next hire most B2B startups need isn't a marketer or a seller. It's an engineer who understands pipeline.
remixes.body:
The next hire most B2B startups need isn't a marketer or a seller. It's an engineer who understands pipeline.

I keep watching the same mistake. Series A company hires RevOps to "fix the funnel." RevOps builds beautiful process docs and Salesforce dashboards. Six months later, nobody's actually built the enrichment pipeline, the outbound automation, or the lead scoring model.

Because those aren't ops problems. They're engineering problems.

A GTM engineer writes the SQL that identifies your best-fit accounts. Builds the Clay table that enriches them with hiring signals, tech stack data, and recent funding. Configures the Lemlist sequences that actually go out. And wires the whole thing together so it runs without manual intervention.

The skillset is specific: SQL, API integrations, some Python or JS, deep familiarity with Clay/Apollo/HubSpot, and enough sales context to know what "good personalization" looks like.

Most importantly, they ship. Not next quarter. This week.

This role will be on every Series A-to-C org chart within two years. The companies that figure it out now are the ones setting meetings while their competitors are still writing process docs.

SQL. Clay. Lemlist. HubSpot APIs. Python scripts. That's the actual stack behind "personalized outbound at scale." And it requires an engineer, not an admin.

The GTM engineer role will be standard in two years. Right now it's an arbitrage. The people who can do this work are underpriced because the market doesn't know what to call them yet.

posts.title: multi-agent is just microservices for AI
posts.hook: everyone's building single mega-agents. same energy as the monolith era.
posts.body:
everyone's building single mega-agents. same energy as the monolith era.

I run a multi-agent system for my daily content pipeline. one agent scrapes trending topics. one agent drafts posts. one agent runs anti-slop detection. one agent handles distribution.

each agent has one job. each agent has its own context. each agent can fail independently without taking down the whole pipeline.

sound familiar? it's microservices. we already solved this architecture problem 15 years ago.

the mistakes I see people making with agents right now are the exact same mistakes we made with monoliths:

✅ cramming too much responsibility into one process
✅ no clear contract between components
✅ one failure cascades everywhere
✅ impossible to debug because everything is entangled

the fix is the same too. small, focused units with clear interfaces.

my content agent doesn't know about my deploy agent. my deploy agent doesn't know about my scraper. they communicate through files and databases, not shared memory.

if you've built distributed systems before, you already know how to build multi-agent. the patterns transfer 1:1.
posts.cta: what architecture pattern from traditional engineering do you see repeating in AI right now?

remixes.label: failure-isolation-story
remixes.hook: My content pipeline went down last Tuesday. Only the distribution agent broke. Everything else kept running. That's the whole point.
remixes.body:
My content pipeline went down last Tuesday. Only the distribution agent broke. Everything else kept running. That's the whole point.

I run a multi-agent system for daily content. Scraping, drafting, quality checks, and distribution are all separate agents with separate contexts and separate failure modes.

When the distribution agent hit a rate limit, the scraper kept scraping. The drafter kept drafting. The anti-slop checker kept catching bad copy. I fixed the one broken piece and everything caught up.

If this were a single mega-agent, the whole pipeline would have stopped. I'd be debugging a massive context window trying to figure out where things went wrong.

This is the same lesson we learned with microservices 15 years ago. Monoliths are easy to build. They're nightmares to debug and maintain. When one thing breaks, everything breaks.

Multi-agent systems give you the same benefits. Independent scaling. Independent failure. Independent deployment. Clear boundaries between concerns.

The patterns aren't new. Service boundaries. Message passing. Health checks. Circuit breakers. We just haven't applied them to AI systems yet because the tooling was too immature.

The tooling is ready now. The architecture patterns have been proven for a decade. The only thing missing is engineers making the connection.
remixes.cta: have you had a single-agent system fail in a way that a multi-agent setup would have contained?

remixes.label: first-principles-analogy
remixes.hook: We already solved the "one giant process doing everything" problem. Twice. First with microservices. Then with containers. Now we're making the same mistake with AI agents.
remixes.body:
We already solved the "one giant process doing everything" problem. Twice. First with microservices. Then with containers. Now we're making the same mistake with AI agents.

The pattern repeats because it's seductive. One agent, one prompt, handles everything. Simple to build. Simple to reason about. Until it isn't.

Your mega-agent is a monolith. It has a single context window that's doing 14 things. When it drifts on task 7, tasks 8 through 14 go sideways too. When you update the prompt for one capability, you risk breaking three others.

I decomposed my content pipeline into four agents. Topic research. Drafting. Quality detection. Distribution. Each has a single responsibility. Each has its own system prompt optimized for one job. Each can be swapped, upgraded, or restarted without touching the others.

The parallels to microservices are exact. Service boundaries become agent boundaries. API contracts become structured output schemas. Health checks become agent status monitors. Circuit breakers become fallback routing.

We have 15 years of distributed systems wisdom. Almost none of it is being applied to AI agent design. The teams that bridge that gap first will build systems that actually work in production, not just in demos.
remixes.cta: what distributed systems pattern do you think is most underused in AI agent design?

tones.advisor:
If you're building AI agent systems, I'd strongly encourage you to study microservices architecture before writing a single line of agent code.

The pattern I see most often: teams build a single large agent that handles research, execution, and validation in one context window. It works in demos. It breaks in production. Because a single failure in any step corrupts the entire pipeline.

The proven approach is decomposition. Each agent gets one job, one context, and one failure boundary. My content pipeline runs four agents. Scraping. Drafting. Quality checks. Distribution. When distribution fails, drafting keeps working. I fix one thing instead of debugging everything.

The architecture maps directly to microservices principles. Define clear boundaries between agents. Use structured outputs as contracts. Implement health checks so you know when an agent is degraded. Build fallback routing so a failure in one agent doesn't cascade.

The teams I see succeeding with multi-agent systems in production are almost always the ones with distributed systems experience. They already know that independent failure domains, clear interfaces, and single-responsibility components are requirements, not nice-to-haves.

Start with two agents. Get the boundaries right. Scale from there.
tones.provocateur:
Your single mega-agent is a monolith. You just don't know it yet because it hasn't broken in production.

It will.

Every generation of engineers makes the same mistake. Build one big thing. Watch it work. Ship it. Then watch it collapse under real-world complexity and spend months untangling the mess.

We did it with monolithic applications. We did it with monolithic deployments. Now we're doing it with monolithic AI agents that try to handle 14 responsibilities in a single context window.

I split my content pipeline into four agents doing one thing each. When one breaks, the others keep running. When I update one, I don't risk breaking the others. When something goes wrong, I know exactly where to look.

This is not innovative. This is microservices 101. The innovation would be if the AI industry actually learned from 15 years of distributed systems wisdom instead of repeating every mistake from scratch.

But that would require AI engineers to respect infrastructure patterns from "boring" backend engineering. And most of them are too busy building impressive demos that fall apart the moment they hit production.

posts.title: outbound personalization that actually works
posts.hook: 40% reply rate lift sounds like marketing. until you see the Clay table that produces it.
posts.body:
40% reply rate lift sounds like marketing. until you see the Clay table that produces it.

I built an outbound enrichment pipeline last quarter that changed how I think about personalization. not the "hey {first_name}, saw you work at {company}" kind. the kind where the email references a specific initiative the prospect's team shipped.

here's the actual flow:

🔧 Clay table pulls new accounts from HubSpot via HTTP API
🔧 Claygent scrapes their recent blog posts + press releases
🔧 Sculptor extracts the top strategic initiative mentioned
🔧 custom formula maps that initiative to our relevant case study
🔧 output column writes a 2-sentence opener referencing their initiative + our proof point

the whole thing runs on a schedule. no human touches it until review.

the lift isn't from personalization tokens. it's from relevance. when someone reads an email that references the project they just shipped, they respond because you clearly did the work.

except you didn't. the system did.

that's the difference between automation that feels lazy and automation that feels informed. the enrichment layer is everything.
posts.cta: no gatekeeping. full Clay table structure + Sculptor prompts in the comments 👇

inspired_by.author: Elena Voss
inspired_by.summary: Details engineering outbound processes with AI-driven personalization and automation to boost reply rates by 40%. Connects sales ops workflows to broader revops goals, including content syndication strategies. Provides a playbook for teams transitioning from manual to automated GTM.

remixes.label: before-after-data
remixes.hook: Our reply rates went from 3% to 11% in one quarter. The emails didn't get better. The research behind them did.
remixes.body:
Our reply rates went from 3% to 11% in one quarter. The emails didn't get better. The research behind them did.

Most outbound personalization is shallow. First name. Company name. Maybe a job title reference. Prospects see through it instantly because everyone does the same thing.

The shift was building an enrichment pipeline that surfaces what the prospect's team actually shipped recently. Not their company's press releases. Their team's specific work.

The Clay table pulls new accounts from our ICP filter. Exa searches for recent engineering blog posts, product launches, or conference talks from people at that company. A Claude prompt summarizes the most relevant initiative in one sentence.

That sentence goes into the email. Not as a compliment. As context for why our solution connects to their current priorities.

"Saw your team launched the new API gateway last month. We've been helping infrastructure teams like yours handle the auth layer so they can focus on routing logic."

That's not personalization. That's research at scale. The difference is that research references something the recipient actually cares about, not something a CRM told you about them.

The pipeline takes 4 hours to build. It runs automatically. And it produces emails that read like a human spent 15 minutes researching each prospect.
remixes.cta: what's the best cold email you've ever received? what made it stand out?

remixes.label: myth-busting
remixes.hook: "Personalization at scale" is a lie. What actually works is research at scale. There's a massive difference.
remixes.body:
"Personalization at scale" is a lie. What actually works is research at scale. There's a massive difference.

Personalization at scale means mail merge with extra fields. Hi {first_name}, I see you work at {company} as a {title}. Everyone does this. Everyone's reply rates reflect it.

Research at scale means finding out what the prospect's team actually shipped last quarter, what problems they're likely dealing with now, and why your solution connects to their specific situation.

The technical implementation is a Clay enrichment table with three steps. Step one: pull new accounts matching your ICP criteria from your data source. Step two: run an Exa search for each account looking for recent blog posts, product announcements, or hiring patterns. Step three: use a Sculptor prompt to extract the one initiative most relevant to your product.

The output isn't a personalized email. It's a research brief. One sentence about what the team is working on. One sentence about why that connects to what you do.

Your SDR writes the email. Or your sequencing tool does. But the intelligence behind it is real, not scraped from a LinkedIn headline.

This is the gap between 3% reply rates and double digits. Not better copywriting. Better input data.
remixes.cta: are your outbound emails personalized or researched? be honest about which one.

tones.builder:
40% reply rate lift sounds like marketing. until you see the Clay table that produces it.

I built an outbound enrichment pipeline last quarter that changed how I think about personalization. not the "hey {first_name}, saw you work at {company}" kind. the kind where the email references a specific initiative the prospect's team shipped.

here's the actual flow:

🔧 Clay table pulls new accounts from HubSpot via HTTP API
🔧 Claygent scrapes their recent blog posts + press releases
🔧 Sculptor extracts the top strategic initiative mentioned
🔧 custom formula maps that initiative to our relevant case study
🔧 output column writes a 2-sentence opener referencing their initiative + our proof point

the whole thing runs on a schedule. no human touches it until review.

the lift isn't from personalization tokens. it's from relevance. when someone reads an email that references the project they just shipped, they respond because you clearly did the work.

except you didn't. the system did.

that's the difference between automation that feels lazy and automation that feels informed. the enrichment layer is everything.
tones.advisor:
The gap between average and high-performing outbound isn't copywriting. It's the quality of research that informs the copy.

Most teams personalize with CRM data. Name, company, title, maybe industry. That produces emails that look personalized but feel generic to the recipient. They've seen it a hundred times.

What actually moves reply rates is referencing something specific to the prospect's current work. A product their team launched. An engineering challenge they wrote about. A hiring pattern that signals a priority shift.

Here's the pipeline I recommend. Start with a Clay enrichment table. Pull accounts matching your ICP criteria. For each account, run an Exa search targeting recent blog posts, product launches, or conference talks. Use a Sculptor prompt to extract the single most relevant initiative.

That gives you one sentence of real context per prospect. Not a compliment. Not a flattery opener. A genuine connection between their work and your solution.

The build takes about four hours. Once running, it processes new accounts automatically. The result is outbound that reads like you spent time researching each prospect individually.

I've seen this approach produce a 40% lift in reply rates across multiple implementations. The delta isn't the email template. It's the input data quality.

Your "personalized" outbound is spam with a first name token. Prospects know it. That's why your reply rate is 2%.

Here's what personalization actually looks like in outbound: referencing something the prospect's team shipped last month. Not their company name. Not their job title. Something they actually worked on.

But that requires real research. And real research doesn't scale with SDRs manually browsing LinkedIn for 10 minutes per prospect. It scales with enrichment pipelines.

I built a Clay table that pulls ICP-matched accounts, searches for their recent work via Exa, and extracts a one-sentence research brief per prospect using Claude. The whole thing runs automatically.

The emails that come out don't sound personalized. They sound informed. "Your team launched X last month. Here's how that connects to what we do." No flattery. No mail merge variables pretending to be personal.

40% reply rate lift. Because it turns out that prospects respond when you demonstrate you actually understand their situation instead of just knowing their name.

The irony is that this takes less effort than manual personalization. Four hours to build the pipeline. Then it runs itself. But most teams would rather have SDRs burn hours doing shallow research that produces shallow results.

posts.title: I mass-deleted my AI infrastructure and rebuilt with SQLite
posts.hook: I was paying for a vector database, a Redis instance, and a Postgres cluster to power a system that serves one user. me.
posts.body:
I was paying for a vector database, a Redis instance, and a Postgres cluster to power a system that serves one user. me.

then I replaced all of it with SQLite on a Mac Mini.

my AI agent system tracks initiatives, stores memory, logs XP, manages content pipelines, and runs cron jobs. the whole thing writes to a single .db file.

no network latency. no connection pooling. no managed database bills. backups are literally cp niobot.db niobot.db.bak.

the discourse around AI infrastructure assumes scale. vector databases for millions of embeddings. orchestration layers for thousands of concurrent agents. GPU clusters for inference.

but most builders aren't at scale. most builders are one person with one machine solving real problems.

I was so caught up in building "the right way" that I forgot to ask whether the right way applied to my situation.

it didn't.

SQLite handles every query I throw at it in under 10ms. the AI agents read and write to it directly. FTS5 handles my semantic search. total monthly infrastructure cost went from ~$80 to $0.

not every problem needs a distributed system. sometimes the boring answer is the right one.
posts.cta: what's something you over-engineered and then simplified? curious what the delta looked like.

remixes.label: cost-audit-reveal
remixes.hook: I added up my AI infrastructure costs last month. $847 for databases and services. Serving a system with exactly one user.
remixes.body:
I added up my AI infrastructure costs last month. $847 for databases and services. Serving a system with exactly one user.

Vector database for agent memory. Redis for caching. Managed Postgres for initiative tracking. Monitoring tools to watch all of it. Each one justified individually. Absurd in aggregate.

So I deleted everything and moved to SQLite on my Mac Mini.

One file. No network calls. No connection strings. No managed service dashboards. No monthly bills from three different providers.

My agent system tracks projects, stores conversational memory, logs activity, manages a content pipeline, and runs scheduled jobs. All of it writes to a single .db file that I can copy, backup, or inspect with one command.

The migration took a weekend. Performance got better because I eliminated network latency entirely. Reliability improved because there are zero external dependencies to fail.

Here's what I learned. We default to distributed infrastructure because that's what production systems use. But "production" usually means "serving thousands of users." When your system serves one user, or even a small team, the complexity of distributed infrastructure is pure overhead.

The right architecture for your scale might be embarrassingly simple. And that's not a limitation. That's an advantage.
remixes.cta: what's the most expensive piece of infrastructure you're running that might not need to exist?

remixes.label: anti-complexity-manifesto
remixes.hook: The best infrastructure decision I made this year was mass-deleting infrastructure.
remixes.body:
The best infrastructure decision I made this year was mass-deleting infrastructure.

I had a vector database because "you need vectors for AI memory." I had Redis because "you need caching." I had managed Postgres because "you need a real database." I had monitoring for all three because "you need observability."

Know what I actually needed? A file.

SQLite replaced all of it. One database file on one machine. My AI agents read and write to it directly. No network hops. No authentication handshakes. No cold start latency from serverless database proxies.

The myth is that simple tools are for simple problems. SQLite handles concurrent reads, supports full-text search, runs analytical queries, and stores hundreds of thousands of records without blinking. It powers more production applications than Postgres, MySQL, and MongoDB combined.

But we don't reach for it because it doesn't feel serious. It doesn't have a dashboard. It doesn't have a pricing page. It doesn't generate DevOps tickets.

That's exactly why it works. Every piece of infrastructure you add is a piece that can break, a bill that can spike, and a dependency that can change its API.

My whole system is one file I can drag to a backup drive. Try doing that with your Kubernetes cluster.
remixes.cta: what infrastructure are you running out of habit rather than necessity?

but most builders aren't at scale. most builders are one person with one machine solving real problems.

I was so caught up in building "the right way" that I forgot to ask whether the right way applied to my situation.

it didn't.

SQLite handles every query I throw at it in under 10ms. the AI agents read and write to it directly. FTS5 handles my semantic search. total monthly infrastructure cost went from ~$80 to $0.

not every problem needs a distributed system. sometimes the boring answer is the right one.
tones.advisor:
Infrastructure decisions should be driven by actual requirements, not industry defaults. And the most common mistake I see is over-provisioning for scale that doesn't exist.

Here's a useful framework. Count your users. If your system serves one person, or a small team, you almost certainly don't need distributed infrastructure. A single SQLite database on a single machine will handle concurrent reads, full-text search, and hundreds of thousands of records with zero network latency.

I was running a vector database, Redis, and managed Postgres to power an AI agent system with one user. Me. The monthly cost was approaching $850 with monitoring included. The migration to SQLite took a weekend. Performance improved because network calls disappeared.

My recommendation: audit your infrastructure against your actual scale. For each service, ask two questions. How many users does this serve? And what would break if I replaced it with SQLite or a local file?

The answers are often surprising. Full-text search with SQLite's FTS5 is fast enough for most use cases. JSON storage works for document-like data. WAL mode handles concurrent access.

Simplicity isn't a compromise at small scale. It's the optimal architecture. Save the distributed systems for when you actually have distributed users.
tones.provocateur:
You don't need a vector database. You don't need Redis. You probably don't need managed Postgres.

I know that's uncomfortable. You spent real time setting those up. You have dashboards. You have connection pools. You have a YAML file that makes you feel like a real infrastructure engineer.

But how many users do you serve? If the answer is less than 100, you're paying a complexity tax for bragging rights.

I ran the full stack. Vectors for AI memory. Redis for caching. Postgres for everything else. Monitoring to watch all of it. $847 a month to serve one user. Me.

Replaced it all with SQLite. One file. On a Mac Mini. Performance went up because I stopped making network calls to read my own data.

The tech industry has a fetish for complexity. We reach for Kubernetes when a cron job would work. We deploy microservices when a monolith would be faster. We spin up managed databases when a file on disk would be more reliable.

SQLite powers more applications than every other database combined. It's in your phone, your browser, and your operating system. But engineers won't use it for "real" projects because it doesn't come with a pricing tier.

Your infrastructure should match your scale. Not your ambitions.

I did something last month that felt reckless and turned out to be one of my best decisions this year.

I deleted my vector database, my Redis instance, and my managed Postgres cluster. All of it. Replaced the entire stack with a single SQLite file on my Mac Mini.

Sounds like a downgrade. It was actually an upgrade. No network latency. No connection pooling. No bills from three different providers. And my AI agent system runs faster than it did before.

I want to walk through why this worked, what SQLite can actually handle (it's way more than you think), and how to figure out if your infrastructure is overbuilt for your actual scale.

posts.title: shipping daily with no team
posts.hook: solo dev shipping every day isn't about speed. it's about removing every decision that isn't 'what do I build next.'
posts.body:
solo dev shipping every day isn't about speed. it's about removing every decision that isn't 'what do I build next.'

I maintain 3 Next.js sites, a content pipeline, an ABM engine, and an AI agent system. alone. no team. no VA.

the trick isn't working 16 hours. it's eliminating decisions.

my deploy command is one word. my content pipeline auto-generates daily posts from a cron job at midnight. my outbound enrichment runs on a schedule. tests run on push.

every manual step I used to do is now either automated or doesn't exist.

the Pieter Levels approach gets memed, but the core insight is real: the bottleneck for solo builders is never coding ability. it's operational overhead.

every time I catch myself doing something manually for the third time, I stop and automate it. not because automation is cool. because my time is the only resource I can't buy more of.

the compound effect is wild. six months of this and my daily output with zero team now exceeds what I produced at companies with 5-person engineering orgs.

not because I'm better. because I eliminated everything that wasn't building.
posts.cta: what's the one manual process you keep meaning to automate but haven't?

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter shares his journey as a solo developer shipping multiple products like Nomad List and Remote OK without VC funding. He emphasizes bootstrapping, iterating quickly based on user feedback, and the power of building in public to gain traction. His approach has led to sustainable revenue streams from indie projects.

remixes.label: systems-over-hustle
remixes.hook: I don't have discipline. I have systems that make discipline irrelevant.
remixes.body:
I don't have discipline. I have systems that make discipline irrelevant.

People ask how I maintain 3 websites, a content engine, an ABM pipeline, and an AI agent system solo. They assume I'm grinding 14-hour days.

I'm not. Most days I start around 9 and close the laptop by 6.

The secret is boring: I spent months removing myself from every repeatable process.

My blog posts generate themselves from a cron job. My deploy is a single command. My content pipeline reads a tracker, drafts across platforms, and queues everything before I wake up.

I didn't build this because I'm productive. I built it because I'm lazy and I hate doing the same thing twice.

Every time I catch myself doing a manual task for the third time, I stop and automate it. Not next sprint. Right then.

The compounding effect is wild. Each automation saves maybe 15 minutes a day. Stack 20 of them and you've bought back an entire workday every week.

Solo doesn't mean doing everything. Solo means building the machine that does everything.
remixes.cta: what's the task you've done manually more than 50 times that you still haven't automated?

remixes.label: decision-fatigue-kill
remixes.hook: the most productive thing I did this year was mass-delete choices from my workflow.
remixes.body:
the most productive thing I did this year was mass-delete choices from my workflow.

I used to start each day deciding: what do I build? which site needs attention? should I write content or ship features?

Now I decide nothing. The system decides.

Monday is infrastructure. Tuesday through Thursday is feature work. Content runs on autopilot, midnight cron. Deploys are one command with zero config decisions.

I didn't get faster. I got simpler.

Barry Schwartz wrote about the paradox of choice in consumer behavior. Nobody talks about it in dev workflows. But it's the same problem. Every micro-decision about tooling, priority, or process burns cognitive fuel that should go toward the actual work.

I run 3 Next.js apps, an outbound engine, and an AI system. No team. The only reason that's possible is because on any given morning, I already know what I'm doing before I sit down.

Productivity advice obsesses over doing more. The real leverage is choosing less.
remixes.cta: how many decisions do you make before you write your first line of code each day?

automate the boring stuff. not as a weekend project. right now. today. the thing you just did manually for the fifth time this week.

posts.title: content strategy is demand gen nobody measures
posts.hook: your best performing outbound emails don't come from better copy. they come from prospects who already saw your content and didn't realize it.
posts.body:
your best performing outbound emails don't come from better copy. they come from prospects who already saw your content and didn't realize it.

I tracked this. pulled HubSpot contact records for every positive reply in Q1. cross-referenced with LinkedIn post engagement data and website visits.

62% of positive replies came from contacts who had at least one prior touchpoint with our content. a post impression, a website visit, a comment on someone else's thread where we were mentioned.

they didn't remember seeing it. but the familiarity was already planted.

this is why content and outbound can't live in separate teams with separate metrics. they're the same pipeline.

the SDR sending 200 emails a day and the content person posting twice a week are working the same deals. they just don't know it.

when I merged these functions, everything changed. content topics were informed by outbound objections. outbound messaging borrowed hooks from top-performing posts. the feedback loop tightened.

if your content team and outbound team don't share a weekly sync, you're leaving pipeline on the table.
posts.cta: how do you connect content engagement to outbound performance? or are they still siloed?

inspired_by.author: Anika Aggarwal
inspired_by.summary: Explores how content strategy drives revenue operations by fueling demand gen and sales enablement pipelines. Details a data-backed approach to content mapping for GTM motions, with case studies showing 3x pipeline growth. Advises on integrating content with sales ops for better attribution.

remixes.label: dark-funnel-receipts
remixes.hook: I found the reason our outbound reply rates jumped 40%. It had nothing to do with the emails.
remixes.body:
I found the reason our outbound reply rates jumped 40%. It had nothing to do with the emails.

I pulled every positive reply from Q1 outbound. Cross-referenced each contact against LinkedIn engagement data and website visit logs.

62% of the people who replied positively had already interacted with our content at least once. A LinkedIn post like. A blog visit. A comment on someone else's thread where we were mentioned.

They didn't reply because the email was good. They replied because they already knew who we were.

This is the dark funnel problem. Your content is doing demand gen work that never shows up in your attribution model. The LinkedIn post from three weeks ago warmed a contact that your SDR thinks they cold-called.

Most teams treat content and outbound as separate functions with separate metrics. But the data says they're the same pipeline.

The teams I see winning right now don't measure content by likes. They measure it by how many outbound touches land on a warm contact versus a truly cold one.

Your content strategy is your outbound strategy. You just can't see it in your dashboard yet.
remixes.cta: have you ever mapped content engagement to outbound reply rates? what did you find?

remixes.label: attribution-is-broken
remixes.hook: your outbound attribution model is lying to you. and it's costing you pipeline.
remixes.body:
your outbound attribution model is lying to you. and it's costing you pipeline.

Here's what most attribution models miss: the prospect who saw your founder's LinkedIn post two weeks ago, visited your site once, then got an SDR email and replied. Your CRM says that's an outbound-sourced opportunity. Your content team gets zero credit.

I audited this for Q1. Pulled HubSpot records for every positive outbound reply. Cross-matched with LinkedIn engagement and site visits.

62% had at least one prior content touchpoint. Not a targeted ad. Organic content they found on their own.

This means your content team is subsidizing your outbound team's numbers, and nobody knows.

The fix isn't better attribution software. It's simpler than that. Start logging content engagement at the contact level, not the campaign level. When an SDR gets a warm reply, check whether that contact engaged with content first.

You don't need a $50k attribution platform. You need a spreadsheet that cross-references two data sources.

The teams that figure this out stop asking "should we invest in content or outbound?" and start asking "how do we make them work together?"
remixes.cta: is your content team getting credit for the outbound wins they're enabling?

Here's what I'd recommend if you're running both content and outbound but measuring them separately: stop.

I ran an analysis on Q1 outbound performance. Pulled every positive reply from HubSpot, then cross-referenced those contacts with LinkedIn engagement data and website visits.

62% of positive replies came from contacts who had at least one prior content touchpoint. They'd seen a post, visited the site, or engaged with a comment thread before the outbound email ever landed.

What this means practically: your content is doing demand gen work that your attribution model can't see. The SDR thinks they sent a cold email. The data says that contact was already warm.

My recommendation: start tracking content engagement at the contact level, not the campaign level. When you get a positive outbound reply, check whether that person interacted with your content first. You don't need new tooling for this. A manual cross-reference of two data sources will give you the signal.

Once you have that data, you can make informed decisions about content investment. Without it, you're flying blind on what's actually driving your outbound performance.

hot take: most outbound teams are taking credit for pipeline that content created.

I have the receipts. Pulled every positive outbound reply from Q1. Cross-referenced with LinkedIn engagement and site visits.

62% of those "outbound-sourced" replies? The contact had already seen our content. Liked a post. Visited the site. Engaged in a comment thread.

That's not cold outbound. That's warm outbound wearing a cold outbound costume so the SDR team can claim it.

Your attribution model is a fiction. It says "outbound" because the email was the last touch. But the content three weeks earlier is what made them open it.

And here's what's maddening: most companies use this broken data to decide budget allocation. Content keeps getting cut because "it doesn't drive pipeline." Meanwhile, it's quietly responsible for the majority of outbound conversions.

If you're a content marketer who feels undervalued, the problem isn't your work. It's that nobody's measuring the thing your work actually does.

Stop fighting about attribution models. Pull the data yourself. Two spreadsheets. One afternoon. The answer will be obvious.

when I merged these functions, everything changed. content topics were informed by outbound objections. outbound messaging borrowed hooks from top-performing posts. the feedback loop tightened.

if your content team and outbound team don't share a weekly sync, you're leaving pipeline on the table.

posts.title: the career pivot nobody warns you about
posts.hook: going from a paycheck to building your own thing is less about courage and more about tolerance for broken things.
posts.body:
going from a paycheck to building your own thing is less about courage and more about tolerance for broken things.

I left a comfortable role to build independently. the first month was great. freedom. autonomy. building what I wanted.

the second month, my deploy pipeline broke at 2am on a Sunday and there was no oncall to escalate to. it was me.

the third month, I realized I was spending more time on invoicing, client comms, and admin than actual building.

nobody posts about month three.

the LinkedIn narrative around career pivots is all month one energy. the leap. the announcement post. the "I finally did it."

but the real pivot happens in the quiet months after. when you're debugging your own accounting spreadsheet at 11pm and wondering if you made the right call.

you did. but not for the reasons you think.

the value isn't freedom. it's ownership. every broken pipeline you fix teaches you something no employer would have let you near. every 2am wake-up builds a skill that compounds.

six months in, I can build, deploy, and sell an entire GTM system end to end. that skill didn't exist when I had a team doing half of it for me.
posts.cta: for those who made the jump... what surprised you most about month three?

He shares lessons on career pivot into sustainable tech entrepreneurship, laying off 15 people to go back to basics, and building products people want by shipping iteratively.

remixes.label: month-three-reality
remixes.hook: nobody talks about month three of going independent. month one is euphoria. month three is a broken CI pipeline at 2am with no oncall to call.
remixes.body:
nobody talks about month three of going independent. month one is euphoria. month three is a broken CI pipeline at 2am with no oncall to call.

I left a comfortable role to build independently. Here's how the timeline actually went.

Month one: freedom. Autonomy. Building exactly what I wanted. Felt like I'd unlocked a cheat code.

Month two: my deploy pipeline broke on a Sunday night. No Slack channel to post in. No teammate to tag. Just me, a stack trace, and a cold cup of coffee.

Month three: I realized I was spending more time on operations than building. Invoicing. Contracts. Infrastructure. DNS issues. The stuff that someone else always handled.

The pivot nobody warns you about isn't the income risk. It's the identity shift. You go from "engineer who builds things" to "person who runs a business that sometimes involves building things."

The skills that got you the comfortable job are maybe 30% of what you need on your own. The other 70% is stuff you never practiced: sales, ops, client management, scope negotiation, knowing when to say no.

I'm not saying don't make the jump. I'm saying prepare for the version of it that nobody puts in their LinkedIn origin story.
remixes.cta: if you went independent, what was your month three moment?

remixes.label: honest-indie-playbook
remixes.hook: the indie dev success stories always start at the highlight reel. here's what the first 90 days actually looked like.
remixes.body:
the indie dev success stories always start at the highlight reel. here's what the first 90 days actually looked like.

Day 1-30: Pure energy. Shipped more in a month than I had in a quarter at my old job. No meetings. No sprint planning. No alignment docs. Just building.

Day 31-60: First infrastructure crisis. Something broke at 2am and the only person who could fix it was me. Started understanding why companies have oncall rotations.

Day 61-90: The real shift. I looked at my calendar and realized I was spending 60% of my time on things that weren't building. Billing. Contracts. Sales calls. Figuring out health insurance.

Nobody tells you that going solo means becoming a generalist overnight. Not a technical generalist. A life generalist. You need to know accounting basics, contract law basics, sales basics. You need to handle your own IT support.

The courage narrative is overblown. I didn't need courage to leave. I needed tolerance for ambiguity and a willingness to be bad at most things for a while.

Six months in, I've found my rhythm. But it looks nothing like what I imagined when I put in notice.
remixes.cta: what skill surprised you the most when you went independent? the one nobody told you that you'd need?

If you're considering leaving a salaried role to build independently, I'd encourage you to plan for the transition honestly.

The first month will feel incredible. Freedom, autonomy, building what you want. That's real, and it lasts.

But around month two or three, something shifts. Your deploy pipeline breaks at 2am and there's no oncall to escalate to. You realize you're spending more time on operations, invoicing, and infrastructure than on actual building.

Here's what I recommend: before you leave, audit how much of your current role is handled by other people. DevOps. IT. Finance. Legal. HR. Sales. All of that becomes your job.

The technical skills that earned you the comfortable role represent maybe 30% of what you'll need. The rest is business operations, client management, and scope negotiation.

Build a 90-day runway that accounts for this learning curve. Not just financial runway. Emotional and operational runway. Accept that you'll be bad at most non-technical tasks for the first few months.

The pivot works. But it works better when you go in expecting the full picture, not just the highlight reel.

the "quit your job and build" narrative is missing about 80% of the story.

LinkedIn is full of indie dev success posts that start at month six. The shipped product. The first paying customer. The freedom lifestyle.

Nobody posts about month three. Month three is your CI pipeline breaking at 2am on a Sunday with zero oncall backup. Month three is realizing you spent the entire week on invoicing, contracts, and DNS issues instead of building.

The courage narrative is a lie. Leaving a job doesn't take courage. It takes a credit card and a resignation letter. What it actually takes is a tolerance for being bad at everything simultaneously.

You're not just a developer anymore. You're a developer plus accountant plus sales rep plus IT support plus project manager plus legal department. And you're terrible at most of those.

I'm not saying don't do it. I did it. I'd do it again. But I'd skip the motivational posts and tell you the truth: the first 90 days are mostly about surviving your own incompetence at non-engineering tasks.

The people who make it aren't the most courageous. They're the most tolerant of broken things.

posts.title: AI in your CI/CD is the sleeper use case
posts.hook: everyone's building AI chatbots. the real productivity gain is an agent that watches your build pipeline and tells you what broke before you check.
posts.body:
everyone's building AI chatbots. the real productivity gain is an agent that watches your build pipeline and tells you what broke before you check.

I wired Claude Code into my deploy workflow. not for writing code. for triage.

when a build fails, the agent reads the error log, identifies the root cause, checks if it's a known pattern from previous failures, and drafts a fix. if the fix is trivial (missing import, type mismatch), it applies it and re-runs the build.

my failed build resolution time went from 15-20 minutes of context loading to about 90 seconds.

the AI isn't writing features. it's handling the interrupts that pull me out of feature work.

this is what I mean when I say the agent conversation is stuck on the wrong question. it's not "will AI write all our code." it's "what are the repetitive cognitive tasks that drain engineers, and can an agent handle those."

build triage. log analysis. dependency conflict resolution. PR review for obvious issues.

none of this is glamorous. none of it makes for a good demo. but it saves hours every week of real engineering time on tasks that require intelligence but not creativity.
posts.cta: what's the most boring but high-impact automation you've added to your dev workflow?

inspired_by.author: Guillermo Rauch
inspired_by.summary: Details new developer tools integrating AI agents into Next.js workflows for seamless automation of builds and deployments. Shares real-world examples of reducing CI/CD times by 70%. Calls for standardized APIs in AI tooling.

remixes.label: ai-triage-not-chatbot
remixes.hook: I stopped asking AI to write code for me. I started asking it to tell me why my code broke. That's where the real value is.
remixes.body:
I stopped asking AI to write code for me. I started asking it to tell me why my code broke. That's where the real value is.

Everyone's debating whether AI can replace developers. Meanwhile I'm using it for the most tedious part of my job: reading error logs.

I wired Claude Code into my deploy workflow. When a build fails, the agent reads the error output, compares it against known failure patterns from previous builds, identifies the root cause, and drafts a fix.

If the fix is trivial (a missing import, a type mismatch), it applies the fix and re-runs the build. If it's complex, it writes up a summary with the root cause, relevant file paths, and suggested approach.

This isn't fancy. It's not agentic coding or autonomous development. It's pattern matching on error logs, which is exactly what I used to do manually except slower and grumpier.

The time savings aren't in the fix itself. They're in the triage. Figuring out what broke and why is usually 80% of the work. The actual fix is often one line.

The boring use cases for AI are the ones that stick. Chatbots are flashy. Automated triage saves me 30 minutes every time something breaks.
remixes.cta: what's the most unglamorous way you're using AI in your dev workflow right now?

remixes.label: ci-agent-recipe
remixes.hook: my CI pipeline has an AI agent that triages failures faster than I can open the logs. here's exactly how I built it.
remixes.body:
my CI pipeline has an AI agent that triages failures faster than I can open the logs. here's exactly how I built it.

The setup is simpler than you'd think. Three pieces:

1. A post-build hook that fires on failure. Captures the full error log and recent git diff.

2. Claude Code reads the error, cross-references against a local pattern file of previous failures, and identifies the root cause.

3. Based on severity: trivial issues get auto-fixed and rebuilt. Complex issues get a written summary with root cause, affected files, and suggested approach.

The pattern file is the key. Every time a build fails and I fix it, the resolution gets logged. After a few months, the agent recognizes most failure modes before I do.

What surprised me: about 40% of build failures fall into repeatable patterns. Missing env var. Type mismatch after a dependency update. Import path changed. These get auto-resolved now.

The remaining 60% still need me, but I get a head start. Instead of reading through 200 lines of log output, I get a three-line summary of what went wrong and where.

Total setup time was about two hours. ROI hit positive within the first week.
remixes.cta: if you could add one AI-powered automation to your build pipeline, what would it handle?

posts.title: sales ops evolved, most teams didn't
posts.hook: the RevOps title changed. the job mostly didn't.
posts.body:
the RevOps title changed. the job mostly didn't.

most "RevOps" teams I see are still doing what Sales Ops did in 2019: managing Salesforce fields, building reports, and firefighting data quality issues.

the title upgraded. the tooling upgraded. the actual capability stayed the same.

meanwhile, the teams winning right now have someone who can:

🔧 build a Clay enrichment table from scratch
🔧 write a Python script to dedupe 50k records
🔧 set up API integrations between their CRM and outbound platform
🔧 build intent scoring models that actually correlate with pipeline

that's not RevOps. that's engineering applied to revenue.

I spent years as an SDR watching ops teams struggle with the same data problems I could see from the front lines. the issue was never process documentation or better Salesforce views. it was that nobody on the ops team could build.

the gap between "manages tools" and "builds systems" is where most revenue orgs lose. they hire operators when they need engineers.

this isn't a dig at RevOps people. it's a dig at how companies staff the function. if your revenue operations team can't write code, you have a reporting team, not an operations team.
posts.cta: does your RevOps team build, or do they configure? genuine question, no wrong answer.

inspired_by.author: David Cummings
inspired_by.summary: Breaks down the evolution of sales ops into modern revenue operations, covering outbound tactics and automation best practices. Shares insights on building cross-functional teams for GTM success and scaling content-led growth. Emphasizes measurable outcomes like CAC reduction and faster sales cycles.

remixes.label: builder-vs-configurer
remixes.hook: I ask one question in every RevOps interview now: 'show me something you built that isn't a Salesforce report.' the silence tells me everything.
remixes.body:
I ask one question in every RevOps interview now: 'show me something you built that isn't a Salesforce report.' the silence tells me everything.

The RevOps role was supposed to be the evolution of Sales Ops. Cross-functional. Strategic. Technical. A bridge between go-to-market execution and data infrastructure.

What happened instead: most companies took their Sales Ops person, changed the title, and called it done.

Same Salesforce admin work. Same report building. Same data cleanup. Now with a fancier LinkedIn headline.

The teams I see actually winning right now have RevOps people who can build a Clay enrichment table from scratch, write a webhook integration without waiting on engineering, spin up a scoring model from CRM data, and prototype an outbound sequence in code.

Not configure. Build.

That's the gap. The RevOps title implies technical capability. Most RevOps hires deliver configuration capability. Those are very different things.

Configuration means you can use the tools someone else set up. Building means you can create the tools your team needs.

The market is slowly figuring this out. GTM Engineering is emerging as the role that RevOps was supposed to be. Whether the title catches on or not, the skill gap is real.
remixes.cta: what's the line between RevOps and GTM Engineering in your org?

remixes.label: revops-career-trap
remixes.hook: RevOps professionals: the title upgrade gave you a raise but it might be trapping your career.
remixes.body:
RevOps professionals: the title upgrade gave you a raise but it might be trapping your career.

Here's the pattern I keep seeing. Sales Ops person gets renamed to RevOps. Salary bumps. Title looks better. Day-to-day work stays identical.

Two years later, the market shifts. Companies start hiring for GTM Engineers who can build enrichment pipelines, write integrations, deploy scoring models, and prototype automations in code.

The RevOps person applies. Gets screened out. Because while the title said technical, the work was configuration.

This isn't a criticism. Most companies didn't invest in upskilling their RevOps teams. They just renamed them and assumed the capability would follow.

If you're in RevOps right now and your daily work is mostly Salesforce admin, report building, and data cleanup, you have a decision to make. Either push to take on technical projects that build actual engineering skills, or accept that the role is Sales Ops with better branding.

The teams winning right now don't have RevOps people who manage tools. They have people who build tools. Who can spin up a Clay workflow, write a custom integration, and deploy an enrichment pipeline without filing an engineering ticket.

The skills gap between "configures platforms" and "builds systems" is growing. And the market is starting to price it in.
remixes.cta: if you're in RevOps, what percentage of your week is building versus configuring?

Let me be direct about what I'm seeing in the RevOps market.

Most organizations renamed their Sales Ops function to RevOps without meaningfully changing the role's scope or capabilities. The title upgraded. The tooling upgraded. The actual work stayed the same: managing CRM fields, building reports, and resolving data quality issues.

The teams outperforming right now have RevOps people who operate at a different level. They can build a Clay enrichment table from scratch. They can write a webhook integration without waiting on engineering. They can spin up a scoring model from CRM data and prototype outbound sequences in code.

The distinction I'd draw is between configuration and building. Configuration means using tools someone else set up. Building means creating the tools your team needs.

My recommendation if you're hiring for RevOps: test for building capability, not tool familiarity. Ask candidates to show you something they created, not something they configured. The Salesforce certification tells you they can operate within a system. You need to know if they can create systems.

If you're in RevOps and want to stay competitive, invest in technical skills. Learn to write basic integrations. Get comfortable with APIs. The role is evolving toward engineering, whether the title reflects it or not.

RevOps is Sales Ops in a nicer outfit. Time to stop pretending otherwise.

I've talked to dozens of "RevOps" teams this year. You know what 90% of them are doing? The exact same Salesforce admin, report building, and data janitor work that Sales Ops did in 2019. They just get paid more for it now.

The title was supposed to mean something. Cross-functional. Strategic. Technical. What it actually means at most companies: "we renamed this person and gave them a HubSpot login."

Meanwhile, a new role is emerging. GTM Engineer. And it's everything RevOps was supposed to be. People who build enrichment pipelines, write integrations, deploy scoring models, and prototype automations without filing a ticket.

Here's the uncomfortable question: if your RevOps team can't build a Clay workflow from scratch, can't write a webhook integration, and can't spin up a scoring model from raw CRM data, what exactly changed besides the title?

The market is starting to figure this out. Companies are posting GTM Engineer roles at senior comp because they're tired of RevOps hires who can configure but can't build.

The title didn't fail. The investment in the role failed. And the people who'll pay for it are the RevOps professionals who were never given the chance to level up.
